coredata.dc:title: The construction of lignin-based carbon dots composites with cold white-light emission and long-lifetime room temperature phosphorescence
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zelin
Liu, Fengshan
Xu, Yang
Li, Huanrong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs)-based room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have attracted widespread attention owing to their excellent optical properties. Especially, profiting from the Stokes shift between fluorescence and phosphorescence, the collaborative work between fluorescence and phosphorescence endows CDs with enormous development potential in the branch of white-light-emitting diodes (WLEDs). However, the precursor of white-light-emitting CDs materials mainly originates from petroleum-derived materials. With the advancement of sustainable development strategies, researchers are increasingly focusing on replacing petroleum-derived materials with biomass resources. Herein, lignin, a biomass material with rich aromatic structure, is selected as the raw material for preparing CDs. A single-component white-light-emitting alkali lignin-based B, N co-doped CDs composites (AL-BNCDs) is prepared by mixing lignin-derived CDs with boric acid (BA) and pyrolyzing at a certain temperature. The luminescence investigations indicate that the synergetic effect of fluorescence/phosphorescence dual emission lead up to the generation of cold white-light emission. The AL-BNCDs exhibit higher fluorescence and phosphorescence quantum yields (QYs) of 35.40 % and 19.87 %, respectively. Moreover, it also has a long lifetime phosphorescence emission of 1094 ms. This study will provide a new approach for the application of lignin-based CDs materials in the fields of WLEDs and anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin-based carbon dots
Fluorescence
White-light-emitting diodes
Room temperature phosphorescence
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of AL-BNCDs in WLEDs and anti-counterfeiting

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support by the Hebei Natural Science Foundation (No. B2021202056) is acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The illustration diagram of AL-BNCDs synthesis process.

(a) The TEM image of CDs inside the composites (inset is the HRTEM image for the corresponding lattice fringes). (b) Particle size distribution of CDs inside the composites. (c) The XRD patterns of AL-BNCDs and thermal annealed BA. (d) FT-IR spectra of CDs and AL-BNCDs. (e) XPS survey spectra of CDs and AL-BNCDs. (f) High-resolution XPS C 1 s; (g) O 1 s; (h) B 1 s and (i) N 1 s spectra of AL-BNCDs.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectrum of AL-BNCDs powders under excitation at 395 nm fitted by Gaussian function. (b) Phosphorescence emission spectrum of AL-BNCDs powders under 395 nm excitation. (c) Fluorescence emission spectrum of AL-BNCDs aqueous solution under excitation at 395 nm. (d) The normalized fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra of AL-BNCDs powders and aqueous solution under 395 nm UV excitation.

(a) The digital photographs of AL-BNCDs under 365 nm UV excitation and after removal of 365 nm UV excitation. (b) Normalized fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra of AL-BNCDs powders under 365 nm excitation. (c) Phosphorescence lifetime decay of AL-BNCDs powders (excitation at 365 nm; emission at 518 nm).
The mechanism diagram of white light emission for AL-BNCDs powders (Ex: Excite, FL: Fluorescence, Phos.: Phosphorescence, Vib.: Vibration).
(a) The digital photograph of the WLEDs lamp. (b) Electroluminescence spectrum of the WLEDs with InGaN UV (395 nm). (c) CIE color coordinate of the WLEDs.

(a) The digital photographs of anti-counterfeiting graphic obtained from
AL − BNCDs
200
350
before and after turning off 365 nm UV lights. (b) The images of information encryption pattern made from
AL − BNCDs
200
350
and
AL − BNCDs
200
400
under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light, and after turning off UV light for 0 s and 7 s.

head.ce:title: The construction of lignin-based carbon dots composites with cold white-light emission and long-lifetime room temperature phosphorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Xiping Road No. 5340, Beichen District, Tianjin 300401, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Xiping Road No. 5340, Beichen District, Tianjin, 300401, P R China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs)-based room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have attracted widespread attention owing to their excellent optical properties. Especially, profiting from the Stokes shift between fluorescence and phosphorescence, the collaborative work between fluorescence and phosphorescence endows CDs with enormous development potential in the branch of white-light-emitting diodes (WLEDs). However, the precursor of white-light-emitting CDs materials mainly originates from petroleum-derived materials. With the advancement of sustainable development strategies, researchers are increasingly focusing on replacing petroleum-derived materials with biomass resources. Herein, lignin, a biomass material with rich aromatic structure, is selected as the raw material for preparing CDs. A single-component white-light-emitting alkali lignin-based B, N co-doped CDs composites (AL-BNCDs) is prepared by mixing lignin-derived CDs with boric acid (BA) and pyrolyzing at a certain temperature. The luminescence investigations indicate that the synergetic effect of fluorescence/phosphorescence dual emission lead up to the generation of cold white-light emission. The AL-BNCDs exhibit higher fluorescence and phosphorescence quantum yields (QYs) of 35.40 % and 19.87 %, respectively. Moreover, it also has a long lifetime phosphorescence emission of 1094 ms. This study will provide a new approach for the application of lignin-based CDs materials in the fields of WLEDs and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasingly severe global environmental issues, sustainable development has become the main theme of global economic development (Kobayashi and Nakajima, 2021; Lozano and García-Verdugo, 2023). Among them, white-light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) have received widespread attention from researchers in the field of sustainable development owing to their long lifetime, small size, low energy consumption and high luminous efficiency (Ma et al., 2020; Ni et al., 2022; Reineke et al., 2009; Zhang et al., 2021, 2023). However, multi-component emitters with different emission colors covering the entire visible light range from 400 to 700 nm are often combined for construction of WLEDs (Kumari and Sahu, 2022; Yue et al., 2023). The disadvantages of poor stability, high cost and complex manufacturing processes of the multi-component emitters limit application and development of WLEDs (Liu et al., 2006; Rajendran et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021a, 2021c; Rohilla and Rao, 2022). Therefore, the development of single-component white-light-emitting materials with simple preparation, low cost and good stability has been the focus of academic research.

According to previous research progress (Li et al., 2021a; Wang et al., 2020b; Zhou et al., 2023b), single-component white-light emission can be achieved through two co-existing emissive centers, in which the short-wavelength emissive center is generated by the lowest singlet excited state, and the long-wavelength center can be generated by the triplet excitonic states of phosphorescence. In addition, due to the long lifetime, large Stokes shift and high energy conversion efficiency of the phosphorescence triplet state, the phosphorescence process has attracted increasing interest from researchers in the field of preparing WLEDs (Wang et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2023). Unfortunately, to date, the phosphorescence materials are mainly concentrated in rare earth metals, organometallic complexes, and non-metallic organic compounds, which suffer from high cost, low thermal stability and complex preparation processes (Li et al., 2023a; Wang and Guo, 2021; Wei et al., 2021). Thus, it is highly desirable to develop low-cost and stable materials to prepare single-component WLEDs.

Carbon dots (CDs) have enormous development potential in a variety of fields, especially in LEDs and room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials, owing to its excellent optical performance, low cost, environmental friendliness and simple synthesis routes (Dordevic et al., 2022; Li et al., 2021b, 2023b). Recently, it has been discovered that CDs materials have fluorescence/phosphorescence dual emissive characteristics, and exhibit significant Stokes shifts between fluorescence and phosphorescence, which has been proven to be promising phosphors for WLEDs (Jiang et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2022a). Wang et al. synthesized a single-component white-light-emitting carbonized polymer dots using urea and phosphoric acid as raw materials (Wang et al., 2020b). In 2022, Li et al. prepared CDs-based composites with ultrabroad-band white-light emission using 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic diimide and boric acid as raw materials (Li et al., 2023c). Although there have been some reports of white-light-emitting CDs works, with the advancement of sustainable development strategies, there is a growing focus on the use of biomass resources to replace petroleum-derived materials as precursors for CDs materials (Atchudan et al., 2022; Luo et al., 2023; Nawaz et al., 2024). Recently, lignin has received widespread attention as the largest renewable aromatic resource in nature. It is mainly composed of three typical structural units (syringyl (S), guaiacyl (G), and p-hydroxy-phenyl (H)) (Chio et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2022b). This rich natural aromatic ring structure endows lignin with great potential for preparing low-cost of CDs. Therefore, the fabrication of single-component white-light-emitting CDs materials with fluorescence/phosphorescence dual emission properties based on lignin will provide a new route for the development of lignin and WLEDs.

Here, we reported a kind of alkali lignin-based B, N co-doped CDs composites (AL-BNCDs) using lignin (alkali) and boric acid (BA) as the raw materials ( Fig. 1). The optical characterization results indicated that the obtained AL-BNCDs exhibited cold white-light fluorescence and long lifetime green phosphorescence emission. The experimental results showed that strong covalent and spatial confine interaction between lignin-derived CDs and BA suppressed the non-radiative transition, which promoted the generation of phosphorescence. Due to the long-wavelength center of phosphorescence emission, the broadband fluorescence emission spectrum appeared, resulting in cold white-light emission. Moreover, the AL-BNCDs exhibited higher fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of 35.40 % and phosphorescence quantum yields (PQYs) of 19.87 %. Based on its excellent optical performance, a WLEDs with Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of (0.29, 0.33), color rendering index (CRI) of 89 and correlated color temperature (CCT) of 7280 K were obtained. In addition, the long lifetime phosphorescence, high quantum yields and low-cost manufacturing process demonstrate the enormous development potential of these AL-BNCDs in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption fields.

Lignin (alkali) (product code: L0082) was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. It was prepared from needle-leaved trees and broad-leaved trees through chemical modifications. Nitric acid (65 %) was provided by Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Boric acid (BA) was obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used without further purification. The deionized water was obtained from an ultrapure water system (ZPE-20) of Tengzhou Zhuopu Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the samples were recorded by FEI Talos F200S system (USA) operating at 200 kV accelerating voltage. The Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the samples were observed by FEI Nova Nano SEM450 (USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of powder samples were measured by a Bruker D8 Discover diffractometer (Germany) scanned at 6°/min in the range from 5 to 90°. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was collected using a Bruker TENSOR 27 spectrophotometer (Germany) with detection range of 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained by a Thermo Fisher K-Alpha (USA). The XPS sample of CDs were prepared using single crystal silicon wafers. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of AL-BNCDs was collected using an Agilent Carry 100 UV–vis spectrometer (USA) with a scan range of 200–800 nm. The steady-state fluorescence spectra, phosphorescence spectra, phosphorescence lifetime and quantum yields were measured by the Edinburgh FS920P with an integrating sphere (UK).

Firstly, 0.1 g lignin (alkali) was dispersed in 10 mL deionized water. The alkali lignin solution was heated in an oil bath at 90 ℃ under stirring at 200 rpm for 1 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixtures and 400 μL nitric acid were transferred to the polytetrafluoroethylene reactor, which was hydrothermal reaction at 200 ℃ for 12 h. After the reaction, the obtained product was centrifuged and then filtrated by a 0.22 μm filter membrane, which was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃.

Briefly, 200 μL CDs solutions (10 mg/mL) and 3 g BA were dissolved into 40 mL deionized water, followed by heating at 90 °C until the water evaporated completely. After that, the samples were grounded into a fine powder, transferred to a crucible and calcined in a muffle furnace at 350 ℃ for 2 h to obtain the final product, named AL-BNCDs.
In order to study the mechanism of phosphorescence, the heated BA was prepared, which was similar with AL-BNCDs preparation process but no CDs addition.

The WLEDs was prepared by using a UV-LED chips with the peak emission wavelength at 395 nm. A certain amount of AL-BNCDs powders were added into epoxy resin AB glue and stirred to mix evenly. Subsequently, the mixture applied to the center of the LED chips and cured at 100 ℃ for 1 h to obtain LED beads.

As illustrated in Fig. 1, the AL-BNCDs were prepared through the heating treatment of boric acid and lignin-derived CDs in a muffle furnace. Surprisingly, the material had both white fluorescence and green phosphorescence emission, showing excellent fluorescence/phosphorescence dual emission performance. Therefore, the structural and optical characterizations were further performed to explore the source of luminescence properties.

Firstly, the morphology and structure of the composites were observed using TEM and SEM. As illustrated in the TEM images, the internal CDs in composites exhibited a quasi-spherical shape and were uniformly dispersed in the BA matrix, with an average particle size of 3.93 nm ( Fig. 2a and 2b). It possessed a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm, which was corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite carbon (Shi et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023, 2020a). The surface morphology of the composites was observed using SEM. As shown in the Fig. S1, it could be seen that the pure BA exhibited a sheet-like stacking structure with a relatively smooth surface, while the composites still had a sheet-like stacking structure after heat treatment, but the surface was relatively rough. Moreover, in the Fig. 2 c, the XRD pattern of AL-BNCDs showed the characteristic peaks of boric oxide (B2O3) at 14.7°, 27.9°, 30.1° and 39.7°, which was according to the PDF NO. JCPDS 06–0297 (Cheng et al., 2022; Jie et al., 2023; Li et al., 2023c). The results indicated that BA was dehydrated during the calcination process, forming amorphous B2O3 matrix. At the same time, the CDs further reacted with BA and were fixed in the B2O3 matrix, which was conducive to the generation of phosphorescence (Feng et al., 2021).

In order to further analyze the chemical properties of the sample, the FT-IR and XPS spectra analysis on CDs and AL-BNCDs were observed. Fig. 2d showed the FT-IR spectra of CDs, BA and AL-BNCDs composites, respectively. In the FT-IR spectra, CDs exhibited absorption bands at 3317, 1660, 1593–1502, 1340 and 1137 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching vibrations of O─H/N─H, C═O, C═C/C═N, C─N and C─O─C, respectively (Hou et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2023a). After heat treatment CDs and BA, the new absorption peaks appeared at 1446, 1195, 1031 and 925 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra, which were correspond to the stretching vibrations B─O, B─O─H, B─O─C and C─B, respectively (Jin et al., 2024; Li et al., 2022). Furthermore, the XPS spectroscopy analysis of CDs and AL-BNCDs were measured and shown in Fig. 2e. The XPS spectra of CDs indicated that it had successfully doped N atoms with a relative content of 6.61 at% (Fig. S2 and Table S1). Due to the influence of lignin through the sodium sulfite treatment, the S signal with atomic content to be 3.25 at% could be observed in the XPS spectra (Latham et al., 2021). In addition, it could be observed that the AL-BNCDs obtained from CDs and BA heat treatment were mainly composed of C, O, B, N and S elements with atomic content to be 19.69 at%, 48.8 at%, 29.71 at%, 1.59 at% and 0.21 at%, respectively. In the Fig. 2f, the high-resolution (HR) C1s spectra demonstrated the peaks centered at 287.5, 286.2, 284.8 and 283.6 eV belonged to C═O, C─O/C─N, C─C/C═C and C─B, respectively (Liu et al., 2023). The HR spectra of O 1 s fitted three peaks at 534.1, 532.9 and 531.6 eV (Fig. 2g), corresponding to the binding energies of C─O, B─O/N─O and C═O, respectively (Zhang et al., 2022). The Fig. 2h exhibited that the HR spectra of B 1 s could be fitted into the three peaks centered at 194.5, 193.3 and 192.1 eV belonging to B─O, B2O3 and BCO2, respectively (Ding et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2023). The N 1 s band contained two peaks at 402.2 and 400.8 eV, which could be attributed to N─O and C═N─C (Fig. 2 i) (Jie et al., 2023). According to the results of XPS and FT-IR, it clearly demonstrated that the CDs were successfully doped heteroatoms with electron-donating N and electron-deficient B, and were firmly fixed by confinement in the B2O3 matrix. Therefore, it showed strong spin-orbit coupling and was conducive to populating and stabilizing the triplet states to promote the efficiency of intersystem crossing (ISC) process (Liang et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2023b), which was the vital factor for excellent phosphorescence.

Interestingly, as shown in Fig. S3, the AL-BNCDs powders exhibited white fluorescence and green phosphorescence emission. To explain this phenomenon, the AL-BNCDs optical properties were characterized. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of AL-BNCDs exhibited a broader absorption band and included the visible light region (Fig. S4). It showed absorption peaks at 220, 262 and 330–365 nm, which attributed to the π-π* transition of C═C and the n-π* transition of C═O, respectively (Cheng et al., 2022). In the Fig. S5a, the fluorescence spectra showed that there was no significant red-shift in the emission of the AL-BNCDs powders with various wavelength excitation, which demonstrated that there were no obvious excitation-dependent characteristics in the AL-BNCDs. The optimum excitation and emission wavelengths were centered at 395 and 478 nm, respectively (Fig. S5b). As seen in Fig. 3a, the emission spectrum appeared as a broad band covering a range from 400 to 700 nm under 395 nm UV excitation. By fitting the Gaussian function, it was found that the emission spectrum showed obvious multiple emission peaks centered at about 430, 477 and 527 nm (Table S2). Therefore, it was speculated that white-light emission might originate from the synergistic effect of multiple emission centers. To further demonstrate this mechanism, the phosphorescence emission spectrum of AL-BNCDs powders were measured under 395 nm UV excitation. The results indicated that the phosphorescence emission spectrum of AL-BNCDs was similar to the emission centered at 527 nm in the fluorescence spectrum of AL-BNCDs powders (Fig. 3b). It is well known that the quenching of phosphorescence usually occurs by dissolved oxygen in water and solvent-assisted relaxation (Li et al., 2023b). Therefore, when the AL-BNCDs powders were dissolved in aqueous, its phosphorescence emission peak at 527 nm were disappeared. The AL-BNCDs aqueous solution exhibited blue emission under 395 nm excitation which coincided with the fluorescence peaks of AL-BNCDs powders of 430 and 477 nm (Fig. 3c and 3d). In addition, the fluorescence emission intensity of AL-BNCDs powders decreased with increasing temperature, as shown in Fig. S6. Thus, based on experimental results and previous research experience (Wang et al., 2020b), the cold white-light emission of AL-BNCDs was triggered by a combination of blue fluorescence and green phosphorescence. Moreover, the AL-BNCDs powders exhibited a QYs of 35.40 % under 395 nm UV excitation, proving its excellent white fluorescence performance.

The phosphorescence performance of AL-BNCDs powders was further investigated. In the Fig. S7a, the AL-BNCDs powders showed an excitation-dependent phosphorescence. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the phosphorescence emission peak exhibited a certain red-shift. The phosphorescence excitation and emission spectra showed that the optimal excitation wavelength was 365 nm, and the highest emission was 518 nm (Fig. S7b). After removal of 365 nm excited, the AL-BNCDs powders could emit green phosphorescence for 14 s in naked eye mode ( Fig. 4a). In the Fig. 4b, it could be observed that there had a significant Stokes shift between the fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra. And, as shown in Fig. S8 , the intensity of phosphorescence decreased with the increase of temperature, demonstrating exclusion of thermally activated delayed fluorescence. The average lifetime of AL-BNCDs powders was 1094 ms, and the PQYs was 19.87 % (Fig. 4c and Table S3). Moreover, the BA materials after thermal annealing exhibited very weak phosphorescence compared to the AL-BNCDs, which indicated the RTP was attributed to lignin-derived CDs rather than single BA (Fig. S9).

In order to further verify the white-light emission and RTP characteristics, a series of control experiments were designed. According to the reaction conditions, the samples were named as
AL − BNCDs
y
x
, where the superscript x represented the reaction temperature (°C), and the subscript y denoted the content of CDs (μL). Firstly, the
AL − BNCDs
100
350
,
AL − BNCDs
200
350
and
AL − BNCDs
300
350
powders were prepared at 350 °C with various volume (100, 200 and 300 μL) of CDs. The Fig. S10a showed the fluorescence emission spectra of
AL − BNCDs
100
350
,
AL − BNCDs
200
350
and
AL − BNCDs
300
350
powders under 395 nm UV excitation. According to intensities of the samples,
AL − BNCDs
200
350
exhibited optimal fluorescence performance. The CIE color coordinates of
AL − BNCDs
100
350
,
AL − BNCDs
200
350
and
AL − BNCDs
300
350
were (0.242, 0.305), (0.264, 0.331) and (0.275, 0.347), respectively (Fig. S10b). It could be observed that the CIE coordinate underwent a certain red shift with the CDs content increased. Similarly, the fluorescence properties of AL-BNCDs prepared at 300 °C with various CDs contents had a similar result to the
AL − BNCDs
y
350
(Fig. S11a). The CIE coordinate of
AL − BNCDs
100
300
,
AL − BNCDs
200
300
,
AL − BNCDs
300
300
were (0.218, 0.293), (0.224, 0.301) and (0.233, 0.318), respectively (Fig. S11b). To explain this phenomenon, the fluorescence spectra was normalized. As shown in the Fig. S12, it was found that as the increase of lignin-derived CDs content, the emission peak intensity at 430 nm became lower and the emission peak at 527 nm became wider. It could be inferred that the change of CIE coordinates might be due to the change of peak width. In addition, it could be deduced that reaction temperature increasing will also widen the emission peak at 527 nm (Fig. S13). To investigate the reasons for the broadening of emission peaks, the phosphorescence spectra were also normalized. The results showed that the increase in temperature and the amount of CDs caused the broadening of the phosphorescence emission peak, resulting in a broadening of the fluorescence emission peak as well (Fig. S14). However, the broadening of the phosphorescence emission peak could be attributed to the increase in oxidation degree caused by reaction temperature rise and the aggregation caused by molecule interactions due to the increase in CDs amount (Ding et al., 2021; Mu et al., 2016). Therefore, it could be inferred that heating and increasing CDs amount had a certain regulatory effect on the CIE color coordinates.

Moreover, to verify this hypothesis, the
AL − BNCDs
200
400
was prepared at 400 °C and 200 μL CDs. As shown in Fig. S15 , its CIE coordinate was (0.233, 0.300). To explain this phenomenon, the fluorescence intensities of
AL − BNCDs
200
300
,
AL − BNCDs
200
350
and
AL − BNCDs
200
400
was compared. The fluorescence intensity of AL-BNCDs gradually decreased with the reaction temperature increased, and the color of AL-BNCDs materials gradually deepened under sunlight (Fig. S16 and S17). Thus, as the reaction temperature increased, the degree of CDs carbonization increased. At high reaction temperatures, excessive carbonization of CDs damaged the structure of the materials, resulting in a decrease in luminescence intensity (Li et al., 2023c).

In addition, the effects of reaction temperature and the CDs content on the phosphorescence performance of the AL-BNCDs were also further explored. Firstly,
AL − BNCDs
100
350
,
AL − BNCDs
200
350
and
AL − BNCDs
300
350
powders were used as model samples to investigate the effect of CDs content on phosphorescence emission. As shown in Fig. S18, the
AL − BNCDs
200
350
exhibited optimal phosphorescence performance, which was consistent with the above results. When the input amount of CDs was 200 μL, the phosphorescence emission intensity of AL-BNCDs gradually decreased with the increase of reaction temperature (Fig. S19). Meanwhile, its phosphorescence decay lifetimes gradually decreased (Fig. S20 and Table S4). It once again proved that excessive carbonization caused by high temperature had disrupted the structure of lignin-based CDs, resulting in a decrease in the optical performance. Therefore, the
AL − BNCDs
200
350
had the most excellent white fluorescence and phosphorescence emission, with high QYs of 35.40 % and PQYs of 19.87 %, respectively.

According to the above data analysis, the origin of AL-BNCDs with excellent white fluorescence and long lifetime RTP had been summarized ( Fig. 5). The FT-IR and XPS results demonstrated that AL-BNCDs had abundant functional groups such as C═O, C─N, and C─B, which exhibited strong spin-orbit coupling ability and could effectively filled triplet states, facilitating the generation of phosphorescence (Liu et al., 2024; Li et al., 2024). Moreover, thermal treatment caused covalent coupling reactions between lignin-derived CDs and BA. Therefore, under the dual effects of heteroatoms and covalent bonds, long lifetime RTP emission was generated. And it promoted the generation of single-component white-light-emitting composed of short-wavelength emission from the lowest singlet excited state for fluorescence and long-wavelength emission from the triplet excitonic states for phosphorescence. Meanwhile, the white-light-emitting could be adjusted by the reaction temperature and the addition amount of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of AL-BNCDs in WLEDs and anti-counterfeiting
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent single-component white-light-emitting and low-cost characteristics, the AL-BNCDs had enormous development potential in the preparing of WLEDs. Typically, a certain amount of AL-BNCDs powders was dispersed into silicone and coated on the center of a 395 nm UV-LED chips to prepare WLEDs. When a voltage of 3 V was applied, the LED beads produced bright white light emission ( Fig. 6a). As shown in Fig. 6b, the electroluminescence spectrum of the WLEDs covered the visible light region and had significant broadband emission. The WLEDs prepared from AL-BNCDs exhibited CCT value of 7280 K, CIE color coordinate of (0.29, 0.33) and CRI of 89 (Fig. 6 c). Moreover, it still showed good stability after being continuously powered on for 1 h (Fig. S21).

In addition, the AL-BNCDs had excellent long-lifetime RTP performance, making it have huge application potential in the fields of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. As shown in Fig. 7a, a security anti-counterfeiting graphic derived from Chinese characters was constructed using
AL − BNCDs
200
350
samples. When illuminated by UV light, the pattern exhibited bright white fluorescence. Green RTP emission appeared when UV light was removed. It had achieved a simple phosphorescence anti-counterfeiting application. Similarly, the information encryption could also be achieved. In the Fig. 7b, the “888” encryption pattern was displayed, with the “120” parts of “888” using
AL − BNCDs
200
350
samples and the
AL − BNCDs
200
400
used for other parts. Under the UV light irradiation, the pattern displayed white fluorescence of “888”. After removing the UV light, the “888” message still appeared in the first second. However, due to the different RTP lifetimes of the two samples, an information of “120” was exhibited after 7 s. The above results indicated that AL-BNCDs composites could be well applied in the fields of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

In summary, we developed a simple and low-cost strategy to prepare single-component white-light emission and long lifetime phosphorescence composites by calcining lignin-derived CDs and BA. The as-prepared AL-BNCDs materials exhibited a high QYs for 35.40 % and PQYs reached up to 19.87 %. Moreover, it also had a phosphorescence lifetime of 1094 ms, with visible phosphorescence emission of 14 s to the naked eye, which was a higher level in lignin-based afterglow materials. The experimental results indicated that N doping and BA matrix assistance played a crucial role in excellent phosphorescence performance. Therefore, the short-wavelength lignin-derived CDs fluorescence and long-wavelength phosphorescence together formed a single-component white-light emission. Based on the fluorescence/phosphorescence dual emission performance, a white LED with CIE coordinates of (0.29, 0.33), CCT of 7280 K and CRI of 89 was constructed and applied in the fields of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. This work will provide a new approach for the efficient utilization of lignin.

Zelin Zhang: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Investigation. Fengshan Liu: Validation, Formal analysis. Yang Xu: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Huanrong Li: Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support by the Hebei Natural Science Foundation (No. B2021202056) is acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.indcrop.2024.119124.
